concerns.text: The Edge Cases section uses a placeholder '[deferred]' for the sample‑size discrepancy threshold ("If narrative text and tabular data disagree on sample sizes by more than [deferred] of the larger count, the entry is flagged ‘size mismatch’"). This leaves the rule un‑operationalisable; a concrete percentage (e.g., 5 %) is required for consistent implementation and testing.

FR-004 references a confidence‑interval level as `[deferred]`. The idea explicitly states a 95 % confidence level; without a concrete value the rule “reported p‑value lies outside the confidence interval derived from the reconstructed test (at [deferred] level)” is ambiguous and cannot be operationalised.

FR-005 mentions a Wilson `[deferred]` confidence interval but omits the confidence level (e.g., 95 %). The idea requires a 95 % Wilson CI; the placeholder prevents verification of the correct interval.

FR-005 adds several statistical analyses (chi‑squared goodness‑of‑fit test, Cochran‑Armitage trend test, Bonferroni‑adjusted subgroup tests) that are not present in the original idea. This constitutes scope over‑reach.

FR-001 (accept list of URLs) is not cited by any user story; the idea’s US‑1, US‑2, US‑3, US‑4 all reference other FRs but none explicitly anchor FR-001, breaking traceability and suggesting a mis‑aligned scope.
FR-002 (automatic extraction) is orphaned – no user story references it, whereas the idea’s US‑2 maps to FR‑002. The missing anchor obscures the intended functional coverage.
FR-003 (reconstruct p‑value) is not linked to any user story, despite the idea’s US‑2 explicitly requiring reconstruction of significance.
FR-006 (export reproducible research package) is not anchored to any user story; US‑3 describes reproducibility but does not cite FR‑006, creating a gap between the idea and the spec.

FR-009 (CPU‑only execution constraints) is orphaned – no user story explicitly references it, even though US‑4 mentions CI constraints. The lack of anchoring weakens the traceability of the CI requirement.

SC-002 (inconsistency‑detection precision ≥ 90 %) is not tied to any user story; the idea’s success criteria only mention precision in the context of US‑1/US‑2. The missing anchor makes it unclear which stakeholder the criterion serves.

SC-003 (all statistical tests must be computed with SciPy) lacks a user‑story anchor; the idea’s methodology sketch mentions SciPy but does not map this criterion to a specific story, breaking the traceability chain.

Beyond the extra analyses already flagged, FR-005 also includes “temporal trends … using a simple proportion‑difference test at α = 0.05” which the idea only alludes to in US‑2. The specification’s detailed sub‑analyses exceed the idea’s stated scope.

The synthetic validation dataset (steps 8‑10 in the Methodology sketch) generates ground‑truth p‑values using the same SciPy implementation that the audit pipeline later uses for reconstruction. Because the ground truth and the system under test rely on identical code paths, any systematic bias or bug in the statistical computation will be shared, violating the independence required for a valid validation. Use an alternative calculation method (e.g., analytical formulas or a different library) to generate the true p‑values, or cross‑validate with hand‑computed cases.

FR‑004 flags a summary as inconsistent when the absolute difference between reported and reconstructed p‑values exceeds 0.05. Absolute p‑value differences are not scale‑invariant; a 0.05 gap is huge for p = 0.001 but negligible for p = 0.8. This threshold can produce many false positives/negatives and is not a standard statistical practice. Consider using a relative difference, log‑scale comparison, or a significance test (e.g., comparing the reported test statistic to the reconstructed one) instead of a fixed 0.05 absolute cutoff.

User Story 3 (Reproducibility Package Export) is not anchored to any functional requirement or success criterion; no FR explicitly mandates the export of a reproducible package and no SC measures its correctness. (Prior concern remains unaddressed)

User Story 4 (Efficient CI Execution) lacks an explicit anchor to a functional requirement or success criterion; FR‑009 describes CPU‑only execution but the story does not reference it. (Prior concern remains unaddressed)

Success Criterion SC‑002 (inconsistency‑detection precision ≥ 90 %) is not tied to any user story; it only cites FR‑004. (Prior concern remains unaddressed)
Success Criterion SC‑003 (statistical tests must be computed with SciPy) lacks a story anchor; no user story references SC‑003. (Prior concern remains unaddressed)

FR‑004 criterion 1 references a confidence‑interval level as `[deferred]`; without a concrete confidence level (e.g., 95 %) the rule is ambiguous and cannot be operationalised. (Prior concern remains unaddressed)
FR‑005 mentions a Wilson `[deferred]` confidence interval but omits the confidence level, making the CI width undefined. (Prior concern remains unaddressed)

FR‑005 adds several statistical analyses (chi‑squared goodness‑of‑fit, Cochran‑Armitage trend test, Bonferroni‑adjusted subgroup tests) not mentioned in the idea; these constitute scope over‑reach without justification. (Prior concern remains unaddressed)
FR-005 mentions computing a Wilson `[deferred]` confidence interval but does not specify the confidence level (e.g., 95 %). Without a concrete level the interval cannot be reproduced or verified.

FR-005 adds several statistical analyses (chi‑squared goodness‑of‑fit test, Cochran‑Armitage trend test, Bonferroni‑adjusted subgroup tests) that are not referenced in the Idea document or any user story, creating scope over‑reach and an internal inconsistency between the specification and the research plan.
Functional requirements FR-001, FR-002, FR-003, FR-006, and FR-009 are not anchored to any user story or success criterion, leaving them orphaned and breaking traceability within the spec.
FR-011 (power‑analysis script) is not referenced by any user story or success criterion; the original idea does not include a power‑analysis requirement, so this adds out‑of‑scope functionality.

FR-005 adds several statistical analyses (chi‑squared goodness‑of‑fit test, Cochran‑Armitage trend test, Bonferroni‑adjusted subgroup tests) that are not referenced in the Idea document or any user story, creating scope over‑reach and an internal inconsistency between the specification and the research plan.

concerns.text: The Edge Cases section uses a placeholder '[deferred]' for the sample‑size discrepancy threshold ("If narrative text and tabular data disagree on sample sizes by more than [deferred] of the larger count, the entry is flagged ‘size mismatch’"). This leaves the rule un‑operationalisable; a concrete percentage (e.g., 5 %) is required for consistent implementation and testing.

FR‑004 defines inconsistency based on a **relative difference > 0.5** (50 %), while Acceptance Scenario 2 flags inconsistency when the **absolute p‑value difference exceeds 0.05**. These two thresholds are contradictory and cannot both be satisfied; the spec must choose a single, coherent criterion.

FR-011 adds a power‑analysis script, which was not requested in the original idea. The idea’s research question and methodology do not call for power analysis, so this functional requirement extends the project beyond its defined scope.
SC-006 specifies a ≥ 80 % power success criterion tied to FR‑011. Since the original idea contains no power‑analysis component, this success criterion introduces an out‑of‑scope metric.

FR-005 specifies computing a Wilson confidence interval but leaves the confidence level as `[deferred]`. Without a concrete level (e.g., 95 %), the interval cannot be constructed or interpreted, making the aggregate analysis under‑specified and potentially invalid.

FR‑001 (accept list of URLs) is not anchored to any user story; no US cites FR‑001, breaking traceability between requirements and scenarios.
FR‑002 (automatic extraction) is orphaned – no user story references it, leaving its purpose un‑linked to a concrete stakeholder need.
FR‑003 (reconstruct p‑value) is not cited by any user story, violating the required FR‑US traceability.
FR‑006 (export reproducible research package) is not anchored to any user story; US‑3 describes reproducibility but does not reference FR‑006.
FR‑009 (CPU‑only execution constraints) is orphaned – no user story explicitly references it, despite US‑4 being intended to cover CI execution.
SC‑002 (inconsistency‑detection precision ≥ 90 %) is not tied to any user story; it only cites FR‑004, breaking the US‑SC linkage.
SC‑003 (statistical tests must be computed with SciPy) lacks an anchor to any user story, leaving its relevance unclear.
The third sub‑criterion of FR‑004 still uses a placeholder ‘[deferred]’ for the relative tolerance, making the rule ambiguous and untestable.
FR‑005 mentions computing a Wilson ‘[deferred]’ confidence interval without specifying the confidence level (e.g., 95 %). The missing level prevents verification of the interval width.

FR-004 criterion 3 still contains a placeholder '[deferred]' for the relative tolerance on metric inconsistencies (e.g., sample‑size or effect‑size mismatches). Without a concrete tolerance value the rule cannot be operationalised nor verified in testing.

The edge‑case description for ‘Conflicting Sample Sizes’ uses '[deferred]' of the larger count as the tolerance threshold. This placeholder makes the detection rule ambiguous and untestable; a specific numeric or percentage tolerance must be defined.

FR-011 adds a power‑analysis script, which was not requested in the original idea. The idea’s research question and methodology do not call for power analysis, so this functional requirement extends the project beyond its defined scope.
SC-006 specifies a ≥ 80 % power success criterion tied to FR‑011. Since the original idea contains no power‑analysis component, this success criterion introduces an out‑of‑scope metric.

SC-003 (statistical tests must be computed with SciPy) is not referenced by any User Story, breaking the required story‑to‑criterion mapping.
FR-004 includes a tolerance clause expressed as "beyond a **[deferred]** relative tolerance"; the concrete tolerance value is missing, making the rule ambiguous and untestable.

responses.response: Unified inconsistency criteria: FR‑004 now flags a summary as inconsistent when the absolute difference between reported and reconstructed p‑values exceeds 0.05 (absolute‑difference rule) and when reported effect sizes fall outside the 95 % confidence interval. Removed the contradictory relative‑difference rule. Updated Acceptance Scenario 2 to match the absolute‑difference rule.
